decision: two-patch defense neutralization (verdict-paragraph defender-pivot rebuttal preempting pole-region retreat + new detail_extra carrying precession argument, OPEN-004 cross-reference, and color-test escape-hatch closure)
related_prior_expansions: Pre-existing detail_verdict_text (already integrated post-c2) — added the post-hoc test paragraph and the three southern-star counter-examples (Canopus, Achernar, Alpha Cen). Curmudgeon C3 hole #2 (Polaris L=1260 vs d=433 inconsistency) was already closed by an upstream patch that updated L_Polaris to ~2,400 L☉ in both detail_evidence and finding fields — verified by quantitative recomputation: with L=2400, d=433ly, Polaris yields M_V=−3.64 → L_V=2433 L☉ from the apparent-magnitude/distance-modulus calculation, matching observed Δm=3.44 within 0.16 mag.

Curmudgeon C3 hole #1 (Gaia DR3-vs-EDR3 citation mismatch) was also already closed in the current detail_verdict_text — the link now points to doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243940, which IS the Gaia DR3 release paper (Gaia Collaboration et al. 2023, A&A 674 A1), not the Riello 2021 EDR3 paper. detail_evidence still uses the cosmos.esa.int/web/hipparcos URL which is independently correct for the Hipparcos reference. No further citation patch needed.

requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.

Defender pivot anticipated. A dome advocate may concede the smooth-gradient framing and pivot to: "the dome model never claimed a continuous declination-dependent dimming gradient — the claim is narrower, that the south celestial pole region specifically lacks a Polaris-equivalent. Canopus, Achernar, and Alpha Centauri are in the southern stellar bulk, not at the pole, so they are not counter-examples." Three counters preempt this. (1) The dome's stated mechanism is geometric and continuous, not point-singular. The dome model derives its predictions from a refractive index n(r) that depends on radial distance from the disc center, with n(r) rising toward the rim (see the dome's own model parameters: disc_radius=20,015 km, refractive index reaching n→28.8 at the rim per the dome's own parametrization). A radial n(r) by construction produces a smooth, continuous declination-dependent extinction in the dome's azimuthal-equidistant projection — not a discontinuous "pole-only" effect. The defender's pivot abandons the dome's own published mechanism in order to save the WIN. (2) Canopus is already pole-adjacent by any reasonable definition. In the dome's own azimuthal-equidistant projection, declination maps linearly to radial distance from the disc center: r = (90° − δ) × (disc_radius/180°). Canopus at δ=−52.7° sits at r ≈ 15,860 km on the disc — about 79% of the way from the disc center to the rim, well into the high-n(r) region where the dome's own model places its strongest "aetheric depth" effect. Sigma Octantis at δ ≈ −89° sits at r ≈ 19,900 km, only about 4% closer to the rim than Canopus. If the dome's mechanism causes a 3.4-magnitude excess dimming at r=19,900 km, no plausible n(r) profile can leave Canopus at r=15,860 km entirely unaffected. Canopus's observed magnitude (V=−0.74) is fully consistent with its intrinsic luminosity and parallax distance, with no residual extinction term. (3) The pole-only retreat proves too much. If the dome retreats from a quantitative n(r) profile to "asymmetry near the south pole region only, no derivation of magnitudes," then there is no longer any predicted value to confirm. The claim becomes "the south pole happens to lack a bright star," which is a coincidence of stellar distribution at this epoch (see precession point below) and not a derivation from dome geometry. A WIN that survives only by becoming unfalsifiable is not confirmed, it is unfalsifiable.
patches.rationale: WIN-033.c3 advocate_mode (defense_survives=3): defender pivots to "asymmetry near the south pole specifically, no smooth gradient implied." Three counters preempt this. (1) The dome's own n(r) refractive index is a continuous radial function (n→28.8 at rim per the dome's own parametrization); a continuous radial mechanism by construction produces a smooth declination gradient in the dome's azimuthal-equidistant projection — the defender's retreat abandons the dome's own published mechanism. (2) Canopus at δ=−52.7° maps to r≈15,860 km on the disc (79% of the way to the rim), only ~4% closer to disc center than Sigma Octantis; if the mechanism dims SOct by ~3.4 mag, no plausible n(r) profile leaves Canopus untouched. (3) The pole-only retreat proves too much: it converts the WIN into an unfalsifiable coincidence-of-distribution claim with no derived value. EXP-299 tracker note specifically called out (a) Canopus δ=−52° is pole-adjacent and (b) the gradient must affect all high-southern-declination stars — both addressed. Defender-pivot paragraph follows the EXP-295/EXP-296/EXP-297 convention of explicitly labeling the rebuttal block.

patches.target_path: wins[id=033].detail_extra — set field from null to a new HTML string carrying (a) precession argument, (b) OPEN-004 cross-reference (dome's own admission of no derivation), and (c) color-test escape-hatch closure.

changes.new:
Precession dissolves the asymmetry. Earth's axial precession, with a period of ~25,772 years, is moving the south celestial pole toward Canopus. By approximately 14,000 AD the south will have a pole star brighter (V=−0.74) than Polaris is today (V=1.98) — the asymmetry the dome cites as confirmation of disc-edge aetheric depth will literally invert. A geometric mechanism that derives from fixed disc-rim geometry cannot produce an effect that flips on a precessional timescale: the disc rim does not migrate. The pole-star asymmetry is a current-epoch coincidence of stellar distribution, not a structural feature of cosmology. (Sources: IAU SOFA precession routines implement the IAU 2006 precession model; the precessional path of the south celestial pole is well-documented to pass within a few degrees of Canopus around 14,000 AD.)

The dome's own OPEN-004 admits no derivation. The dome model's own published documentation (raw-text/05-model.txt OPEN-004) lists "Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived" as an unsolved open problem. The geometric basis for any pole-related stellar effect has never been worked out by the dome author, on the dome's own published acknowledgment. A WIN that is "CONFIRMED" against an underived expectation is, by definition, retrodictive: there is no quantitative expectation to confirm or falsify, only an observation being relabeled as a success. This is consistent with our broader Part 3 finding that the dome's "WIN" classification routinely conflates retrodiction with prediction.

Color test (wavelength-dependent extinction). Any high-n medium with finite scattering should produce wavelength-dependent extinction (Rayleigh λ⁻⁴ for sub-wavelength scatterers, Mie for larger). If aetheric depth at the disc edge dims southern stars, those stars should appear systematically redder than their spectral classifications predict — i.e., positive E(B−V) color excess concentrated at high southern declination. Hipparcos B−V color indices and Gaia DR3 BP−RP photometry show no such hemispheric color asymmetry: stars' colors track their spectral types at all declinations within photometric precision. A dome defender cannot retreat from this argument by invoking "random stellar distribution," because intrinsic color is set by spectral type and is not a function of which stars happen to lie at the pole at this epoch.

patches.uniqueness_verified: detail_extra is currently null (verified 2026-05-10 against clean clone of origin/main); set operation is unambiguous.
patches.rationale: WIN-033.c3 hole #4 (minor, carryover from c2 unaddressed): "add precession argument to detail_extra (currently null)." Curmudgeon C3 stronger_arguments #1 (color test): converts a brightness argument (where defenders can hand-wave "random distribution") into a color argument (intrinsic to spectral type, not subject to distribution-luck rebuttal). Curmudgeon C3 stronger_arguments #3: dome's own OPEN-004 (raw-text/05-model.txt line 98) explicitly flags "Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived" as unsolved, making the WIN's CONFIRMED status against an underived expectation a definitionally retrodictive claim. detail_extra was null prior to this patch, so a single field_set creates the field and accommodates all three sub-points. The color test gives the review a third line of attack the defender cannot answer with "random distribution."

cross_page_consistency_after_patches.docs_index_html: WIN-033 in walkthrough is generated from data/wins.json by build.js. Build.js will regenerate index.html and pick up the new detail_verdict_text and detail_extra. No separate edit needed.
cross_page_consistency_after_patches.docs_dashboard_html: Dashboard does not contain WIN-033 detail prose (only the WIN list / verdict-bucket summary). No cross-page edit needed.
cross_page_consistency_after_patches.tldr_alignment: tldr_evidence ("Polaris is a supergiant ... no dome geometry needed. Gaia data for 1.8 billion stars shows no systematic magnitude anomalies near the celestial poles") and tldr_verdict ("The magnitude difference is fully explained by stellar classification ... Gaia sees no pole-dependent brightness anomaly across 1.8 billion stars") already correctly characterize the verdict. The new defender-pivot paragraph and detail_extra extend rather than contradict the existing TLDRs (precession is a deepening of the same point; the gradient/color arguments reinforce "no pole-dependent anomaly"). No TLDR change required.
cross_page_consistency_after_patches.cross_win_consistency: Patch is consistent with related WINs. WIN-017 (parallax) cites Gaia DR3 with the same DOI; WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess) discusses a different Polaris property (positional offset from pole, not brightness). Cross-references in the new detail_extra to OPEN-004 (raw-text/05-model.txt) and to Part 3 retrodiction-vs-prediction framing are existing site anchors. The Hipparcos B−V and Gaia DR3 BP−RP color references invoke standard catalogs cited elsewhere on the site.

holes_addressed.hole_summary: Missing explicit gradient/preemption argument. Our text lists three southern stars as counter-examples but never frames the dome's implicit prediction as "a smooth declination-dependent extinction gradient driven by n(r)," leaving room for a defender to retreat to a pole-only formulation.
holes_addressed.how_addressed: P1 "Defender pivot anticipated" paragraph counter (1) explicitly cites the dome's own n(r) parametrization (n→28.8 at rim, radial function) and notes that a continuous radial mechanism by construction yields a smooth declination gradient. The retreat to pole-only abandons the dome's own published mechanism. Canopus's r≈15,860 km on the disc (79% to rim) is computed from the dome's own azimuthal-equidistant projection, showing the gradient cannot leave Canopus untouched.

holes_addressed.hole_summary: Missing precession argument. Earth's axial precession (~25,772 yr cycle) is moving the south celestial pole toward Canopus; by ~14,000 AD the south will have a pole star brighter than Polaris is now. A geometric aetheric mechanism tied to fixed disc-rim geometry cannot produce an effect that flips on a precessional timescale.
holes_addressed.how_addressed: P2 detail_extra paragraph 1 ("Precession dissolves the asymmetry") covers exactly this — explicit ~25,772-yr cycle, explicit Canopus V=−0.74 vs Polaris V=1.98 inversion, explicit appeal to fixed disc rim. Sources: IAU SOFA / IAU 2006 precession routines.

holes_addressed.hole_summary: Wording weakness in post-hoc paragraph: "we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value" sounds like "we looked and couldn't find one," rebuttable by an OTS hash post-dating the catalog data.
holes_addressed.how_addressed: Not modified in this EXP. The current text is internally clear that the data "had been in the public record before the dome's earliest dated commit" and that "no monitor.py entry exists." Tightening the search-effort framing further is a polish item; deferring rather than risking wording regression. Future minor edit can adopt the curmudgeon's recommended phrasing if desired ("catalogued for centuries / Hipparcos/Gaia public for decades / no monitor.py entry").

holes_addressed.hole_summary: Dome defender retreats from "smooth aetheric extinction gradient" to "asymmetry near the pole specifically." Our text addresses it implicitly via three counter-examples but never explicitly states what a real aetheric extinction model should predict (a continuous gradient).
holes_addressed.how_addressed: P1 paragraph addresses all three prongs. Counter (1): the dome's published n(r) IS a continuous radial function — the smooth gradient is the dome's own implicit prediction, not a strawman. Counter (2): explicit r-coordinate calculation showing Canopus is 79% of the way to the rim, falsifying the "not pole-adjacent" pivot. Counter (3): the pole-only retreat converts the WIN to unfalsifiable coincidence — explicit framing of why this is a problem.

holes_addressed.hole_summary: Wavelength-dependent extinction (color) test: any high-n medium with finite scattering produces wavelength-dependent extinction. Hipparcos B−V and Gaia DR3 BP−RP show no hemispheric color asymmetry. Converts brightness argument (where defenders hand-wave "random distribution") into color argument (intrinsic to spectral type).
holes_addressed.how_addressed: P2 detail_extra paragraph 3 ("Color test") states the wavelength-dependent extinction expectation, the photometric absence of hemispheric color asymmetry, and explicitly notes the defender cannot retreat to "random stellar distribution" because color is intrinsic to spectral type. Adds a third independent line of attack to the existing brightness/spectral-type/post-hoc set.

holes_addressed.hole_summary: The dome's own published model (raw-text/05-model.txt line 98) lists "OPEN-004: Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived" as an unsolved open problem. The geometric basis for any pole-related stellar effect has never been worked out by the dome author.
holes_addressed.how_addressed: P2 detail_extra paragraph 2 ("The dome's own OPEN-004 admits no derivation") makes this argument explicitly: a WIN "CONFIRMED" against an underived expectation is by definition retrodictive.

holes_not_addressed.hole_severity: moderate (WIN-033.c3 hole #1) — Gaia DR3-vs-EDR3 citation mismatch
holes_not_addressed.reason: Already closed in the current detail_verdict_text. The link is doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243940, which is the Gaia DR3 release paper (Gaia Collaboration et al. 2023, A&A 674 A1) — not the Riello 2021 EDR3 paper. The hole was raised against an earlier text version. detail_evidence still cites Hipparcos via cosmos.esa.int — independently correct.

holes_not_addressed.hole_severity: moderate (WIN-033.c3 hole #2) — Polaris L=1,260 vs d=433 ly internal inconsistency
holes_not_addressed.reason: Already closed by upstream patch updating L_Polaris to ~2,400 L☉ in both finding and detail_evidence fields. Quantitative verification: with L=2,400 L☉ and d=433 ly, the F ∝ L/d² calculation predicts Δm=3.60 mag vs observed Δm=3.44 mag, agreement within 0.16 mag (acceptable rounding given the 2,000–2,500 L☉ band cited in the literature for d=433 ly Polaris).

new_evidence_added: Inline derivation of Canopus's radial position on the dome's own azimuthal-equidistant disc: r = (90° − δ) × (disc_radius/180°) yields r ≈ 15,860 km at δ=−52.7°, vs r ≈ 19,900 km for Sigma Octantis at δ ≈ −89°. Canopus is 79% of the way from disc center to rim — well within the high-n(r) region where the dome's own model places its strongest aetheric effect. This is computed from the dome's own published parameter (disc_radius=20,015 km).

Explicit citation of the dome's own n(r)→28.8 at the rim parametrization, demonstrating that the dome's mechanism is by construction a continuous radial function — the smooth gradient framing is the dome's own implicit prediction, not a strawman imposed by the review.

Precession quantification: ~25,772-yr cycle, Canopus (V=−0.74) becomes the south pole star around 14,000 AD, brighter than Polaris is today (V=1.98). IAU SOFA precession routines as the standard implementation reference.

Color-test prediction: high-n medium → wavelength-dependent extinction (Rayleigh λ⁻⁴ regime for sub-wavelength scatterers, Mie for larger) → positive E(B−V) excess at high southern declination. Photometric absence in Hipparcos B−V and Gaia DR3 BP−RP refutes any aetheric-extinction model regardless of stellar distribution.

Cross-reference to dome's own raw-text/05-model.txt OPEN-004 ("Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived"), establishing that the dome itself acknowledges no derivation exists.

anticipated_objections.objection: "Your r-coordinate calculation assumes the dome's azimuthal-equidistant projection literally maps declination linearly to disc radius. The dome may deny this exact mapping."
anticipated_objections.response: The dome's own model parameters and published geometry (disc_radius=20,015 km, sun_altitude=5,733 km, firmament_height=9,086 km) are constructed precisely so that an azimuthal-equidistant projection of the spherical sky reproduces observed star positions. This is the standard flat-disc-cosmology projection (used by every flat-disc model since the 1880s) and is what the dome MUST use to reproduce the observed celestial geometry — there is no other projection that maps the celestial sphere to a flat disc and preserves the observed sky. If the dome denies this projection, it cannot reproduce the basic appearance of the night sky (constellations, ecliptic, celestial equator) at any latitude. r = (90° − δ) × (disc_radius/180°) is therefore not an externally imposed assumption — it is the only projection consistent with the dome's own claimed reproduction of observational astronomy.

anticipated_objections.objection: "Precession is itself a globe-derived phenomenon; you cannot use precession to refute a flat-disc model that has its own time-evolution mechanism."
anticipated_objections.response: The dome model has not published a precession mechanism of its own. raw-text/05-model.txt OPEN-004 explicitly flags this as unsolved. Without a published dome precession mechanism, the only available time-evolution model is the standard one (IAU 2006), which is empirically calibrated against millennia of historical pole-star observations (e.g., Thuban as the north pole star ~3000 BC, Kochab/Pherkad ~1500 BC, Polaris current). The dome must EITHER accept the standard precession model (in which case ~14,000 AD Canopus-as-south-pole-star follows) OR publish its own mechanism that produces a fixed-pole geometry inconsistent with the historical pole-star sequence. The dome has done neither. The argument is therefore: under any time-evolution model that reproduces the historical pole-star record, the south pole moves toward Canopus on a 25,772-yr cycle, and a fixed-disc-rim aetheric mechanism cannot produce a flipping asymmetry.

anticipated_objections.objection: "The color test is unfair because the dome's n(r) might not be a Rayleigh-style scatterer — it could be a non-dispersive index."
anticipated_objections.response: A non-dispersive refractive index (n(r) constant in λ) does not produce extinction at all — it would only bend light without dimming. If the dome appeals to non-dispersive n(r) to escape the color test, it must abandon the dimming mechanism entirely (since dimming requires either absorption or scattering, both of which are wavelength-dependent in physical media). The dome cannot have it both ways: any mechanism that dims southern stars must be wavelength-dependent and therefore detectable in B−V or BP−RP excess. Hipparcos and Gaia photometric residuals show no such excess at high southern declination — this is a genuine constraint on any extinction model, dome or otherwise.

kernel_of_truth.statement: The observation that the south celestial pole lacks a Polaris-equivalent bright star is real and historically meaningful. Sigma Octantis at V=5.42 made southern celestial navigation harder for centuries before magnetic and electronic instruments. The dome correctly identifies a fact most readers would not notice without prompting, and its stated mechanism (aetheric depth at disc edge) is at least a candidate explanation that engages the question.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three independent failure modes survive after granting the kernel. (1) The asymmetry is fully explained by intrinsic stellar luminosity: Polaris is an F7Ib supergiant at L_V≈2,400 L☉; Sigma Octantis is an F0III/IV subgiant at L_V≈40 L☉; the predicted apparent-magnitude difference is Δm≈3.60 mag against observed 3.44 mag, with no residual to attribute to dome geometry. (2) The dome's own published mechanism (continuous n(r) refractive index) by construction predicts a smooth declination-dependent extinction gradient, but Hipparcos and Gaia DR3 photometry of 1.8 billion stars show no such gradient at the 1% level — and Canopus at δ=−52.7° (r≈15,860 km, 79% of the way from disc center to rim) is brilliant at V=−0.74, falsifying any plausible n(r) profile. The pole-only retreat from the gradient prediction abandons the dome's own published mechanism. (3) Earth's axial precession (~25,772-yr cycle) is moving the south pole toward Canopus; by ~14,000 AD the asymmetry inverts. A geometric mechanism tied to fixed disc-rim geometry cannot produce a flipping asymmetry. The observation is real; the dome's explanation is not the only candidate, is internally inconsistent, and cannot survive the dome's own model documents (OPEN-004) admitting no derivation exists.

test_recommendation: tests/run.sh after build.js html should pass. The patches are prose-only (no DATA blob, no numeric counts, no rate fields, no classification fields) so existing numeric-claim tests are not affected. Recommend a future test addition: a unit test that detail_verdict_text contains the string "Defender pivot anticipated" for WIN-033 — mirrors the EXP-296/EXP-297 test recommendation and guards against silent regression. Also recommend a test that detail_extra is non-null for WIN-033 (currently null prior to this patch).

closure_recommendation_for_decider.closure_note: EXP-299 P1 (find/replace adding "Defender pivot anticipated" paragraph to WIN-033.detail_verdict_text) preempts the pole-region defender pivot via three counters: (1) the dome's own n(r) is a continuous radial function and predicts a smooth gradient by construction; (2) Canopus at δ=−52.7° maps to r≈15,860 km (79% to rim) on the dome's own azimuthal-equidistant disc, falsifying the "not pole-adjacent" claim; (3) the pole-only retreat converts the WIN to unfalsifiable coincidence. EXP-299 P2 (field_set on detail_extra from null to new HTML) carries the precession argument (~25,772-yr cycle, Canopus-as-south-pole at ~14,000 AD), the OPEN-004 cross-reference (dome's own admission of no derivation), and the color-test escape-hatch closure (any n(r) extinction must produce hemispheric color excess — none observed in Hipparcos/Gaia photometry). After patch integration, the C3 holes #3, #4 (carryover) and stronger_arguments #1, #3 are fully closed, and the ISS-1920 defender-pivot pattern is structurally rebutted in the page text. ISS-1920 should close to fixed.
closure_recommendation_for_decider.notes_for_decider: C3 holes #1 (Gaia DR3-vs-EDR3 citation) and #2 (Polaris L=1260 vs d=433ly inconsistency) are already closed in the current text — verify by reading detail_verdict_text and finding before applying these patches. Hole #5 (post-hoc wording polish) is left for a future minor edit; not addressed in this EXP.

scope_note: Two-patch defense expansion (matches EXP-296 single-patch + a new field_set for the previously-null detail_extra; matches EXP-297 two-patch convention). Justified because the WIN-033.c3 review identified two distinct content gaps: (a) advocate_mode preemption in detail_verdict_text (P1) and (b) factual content additions to detail_extra (P2 — precession + OPEN-004 + color test). Combining them into a single patch would have produced a less-auditable diff; splitting them keeps the rhetorical layer (P1) separate from the factual-content layer (P2).
tldr: ISS-1920 / WIN-033.c3 (defense_survives=3) defender pivot — "the dome never claimed a smooth gradient; only the south pole region specifically lacks a bright star; Canopus, Achernar, Alpha Cen are not at the pole" — preempted by two-patch sequence. P1 adds labeled "Defender pivot anticipated" paragraph to detail_verdict_text with three counters (dome's own n(r) IS continuous and radial by construction; Canopus at δ=−52.7° = r≈15,860 km is 79% of the way to the rim on the dome's own disc; pole-only retreat proves too much by becoming unfalsifiable). P2 sets previously-null detail_extra to new HTML with precession argument (~25,772-yr cycle dissolves the asymmetry by ~14,000 AD), OPEN-004 cross-reference (dome admits no derivation), and color-test escape-hatch closure (any n(r) extinction must produce hemispheric color excess — Hipparcos and Gaia DR3 see none). C3 holes #1 (Gaia DR3 citation) and #2 (Polaris L=1260) already closed in current text — verify before applying.
